\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Encoding Properties}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.Switching.EncodingProperties
\section{Overview}

This module collects the stability, length-bound, and independence lemmas used in
Razborov's encoding argument for the switching lemma. They describe the behaviour of
the low-level routines \lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits},
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}, and \lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovDecode} on
restrictions $\rho_0$, $\sigma$, decision-tree paths, and clause literals, culminating
in the technical facts needed for the encode/decode round-trip.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Declarations}

\begin{lemma}[Output bound for processClauseLits]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_bound}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
For any literal/index list and any path, the combined output of
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} is bounded: the length of its auxiliary entry
list plus twice the length of its remaining path is at most $2 \cdot |\mathrm{path}|$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Tight bound for non-empty inputs]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_tight}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_bound}
On non-empty literal and path lists, \lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} satisfies
the strengthened bound: the auxiliary-entry length plus one, plus twice the remaining
path length, is still at most twice the length of the input path.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Length bound for the encoder loop]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_aux_length_bound}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_tight}
The output produced by the encoder loop \lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}'s
\texttt{go} grows in a controlled way: its second component has length at most
$|\mathrm{acc}| + 2 \cdot |\mathrm{path}|$, where $\mathrm{acc}$ is the accumulator and
$\mathrm{path}$ the current path.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoding length is at most $2d$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_aux_length_le}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DecisionTree.deepPath, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.canonicalDTree, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_aux_length_bound, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}
For a bad restriction $\rho$ of a DNF $f$ with depth parameter $d$, the encoded output
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode} has length at most $2d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits preserves $\sigma$ off the literal list]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_stable}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If a variable $v$ is the variable of no literal in the input list, then
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} leaves the $\sigma$ restriction unchanged at
$v$; that is, the resulting $\sigma$ agrees with the input $\sigma$ on $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits preserves $\rho_0$ off the literal list]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_stable}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If a variable $v$ is the variable of no literal in the input list, then
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} leaves the $\rho_0$ restriction unchanged at
$v$; the resulting $\rho_0$ agrees with the input $\rho_0$ on $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits never frees $\rho_0$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_acc, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If $\rho_0\,v \neq \mathrm{none}$, then after running
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} the resulting $\rho_0$ at $v$ is still not
$\mathrm{none}$; the routine never turns a set value into an unset one.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder $\gamma$ preserves $\sigma$ at non-free variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_nonfree}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_acc, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_stable}
If $\rho_0\,v \neq \mathrm{none}$ (so $v$ is non-free), then the first component
$\gamma$ of the encoder loop \lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}'s \texttt{go}
returns $\sigma\,v$ at $v$; the encoder does not alter $\sigma$ on non-free variables.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Accumulator decomposition for the encoder loop]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_acc}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_acc}
The encoder loop \lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}'s \texttt{go} run with an
accumulator decomposes into a run on the empty accumulator: its result can be expressed
in terms of the corresponding empty-accumulator computation together with the supplied
accumulator.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder $\gamma$ independent of the accumulator]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_acc}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_acc}
The first component of the encoder loop \lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}'s
\texttt{go} does not depend on the accumulator: running with accumulator $\mathrm{acc}$
yields the same first component as running with the empty accumulator.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processEntries preserves none]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processEntries_preserves_none}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, Term}
If $\sigma\,v = \mathrm{none}$, then the decoder's \texttt{processEntries} routine leaves
$v$ unset; the resulting $\sigma$ is still $\mathrm{none}$ at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Decoder loop preserves none]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.decode_go_preserves_none}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processEntries_preserves_none}
If $\sigma\,v = \mathrm{none}$, then the decoder loop
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovDecode}'s \texttt{go} leaves $v$ unset; its resulting
$\sigma$ is still $\mathrm{none}$ at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits components independent of $\sigma$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_indep}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
The remaining path, the $\rho_0$ output, and the auxiliary entry list produced by
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} are all independent of the input $\sigma$: for
any two starting restrictions $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ these three components coincide.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder output independent of $\sigma$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_snd_sigma_indep}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_acc, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_indep}
The second component (the emitted bit list) of the encoder loop
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}'s \texttt{go} does not depend on the $\sigma$
argument: starting from $\sigma_1$ or $\sigma_2$ gives the same output.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Auxiliary entries come from input literals]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_entries_from_lits}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
Every entry $e$ in the auxiliary list produced by
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} has its index $e.1$ equal to the index of some
literal $li$ from the input list.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[No auxiliary entry targets a non-free variable]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_ne_nonfree}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_entries_from_lits, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
Suppose every input literal pair occurs in $t.\texttt{zipIdx}$ and no input literal has
variable $v$. Then for every auxiliary entry $e$ of
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}, the literal at position $e.1$ in $t$ does not
have variable $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Auxiliary entries reference free variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_vars_free}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_entries_from_lits, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
Suppose every input literal pair occurs in $t.\texttt{zipIdx}$ and all input literals are
free under $\rho_0$. Then for every auxiliary entry $e$ of
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}, the literal at position $e.1$ in $t$ has a
variable that is free under $\rho_0$ (its $\rho_0$ value is $\mathrm{none}$).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\sigma$-foldl stable off targeted variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.foldl_sigma_stable}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, Term}
Folding the decoder's $\sigma$-update over a list of entries leaves $v$ unchanged when
no entry targets $v$; that is, if for every entry the literal at its position in $t$ does
not have variable $v$, the folded $\sigma$ agrees with the initial $\sigma$ at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\rho_0$-foldl stable off targeted variables]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.foldl_rho_stable}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, Term}
Folding the decoder's $\rho_0$-update over a list of entries leaves $v$ unchanged when
no entry targets $v$; if for every entry the literal at its position in $t$ does not have
variable $v$, the folded $\rho_0$ agrees with the initial $\rho_0$ at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\sigma$-foldl preserves none]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.foldl_sigma_preserves_none}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, Term}
If $\sigma\,v = \mathrm{none}$, then folding the decoder's $\sigma$-update over any list
of entries still gives $\mathrm{none}$ at $v$; the fold never sets a previously unset
variable to a value via the $\sigma$-update.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\sigma$-foldl produces none at a newly set free variable]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_foldl_sigma_none}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.foldl_sigma_preserves_none, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
Suppose every input literal pair occurs in $t.\texttt{zipIdx}$, that $\rho_0\,v =
\mathrm{none}$, and that \lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} sets the $\rho_0$
component at $v$. Then folding the decoder's $\sigma$-update over the auxiliary entries
yields $\mathrm{none}$ at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\rho_0$-foldl agrees with processClauseLits output]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_foldl_rho_eq}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
Suppose every input literal pair occurs in $t.\texttt{zipIdx}$ and the decoder's initial
$\rho_0$ value agrees with the encoder's at $v$. Then folding the decoder's
$\rho_0$-update over the auxiliary entries of
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} reproduces the $\rho_0$ component of that
routine at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\rho_0$-foldl agrees when $v$ is newly set]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_foldl_rho_eq_of_set}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_foldl_rho_eq, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_no_target_of_rho_none, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
A variant of the previous lemma allowing the decoder's initial $\rho_0$ to differ from
the encoder's: provided $\rho_0\,v = \mathrm{none}$ and
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} sets the $\rho_0$ component at $v$, the
$\rho_0$-fold over the auxiliary entries still reproduces the routine's $\rho_0$ value at
$v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[No auxiliary entry targets a still-free variable]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_no_target_of_rho_none}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none}
Contrapositive of $\rho_0$-stability: if $\rho_0\,v = \mathrm{none}$, the
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} output keeps $\rho_0$ unset at $v$, and there
are at least as many path entries as literals, then no input literal has variable $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits $\sigma$ output depends only on $\sigma\,v$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_at_v}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If two starting restrictions agree at $v$ ($\sigma_1\,v = \sigma_2\,v$), then the
$\sigma$ output of \lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} at $v$ is the same for both;
the value at $v$ depends only on the initial $\sigma$ at $v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[$\sigma$ stays none when $\rho_0$ stays free]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_none_of_rho_none}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none}
If $\rho_0\,v = \mathrm{none}$ and \lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} leaves
$\rho_0$ unset at $v$, then it also leaves $\sigma$ unchanged at $v$; the $\sigma$ output
equals the input $\sigma$ at $v$, since $\rho_0$ and $\sigma$ are updated at the same
variables in lockstep.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder $\gamma$ at a free variable independent of initial $\sigma$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_sigma_indep_at_free}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_acc, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_nonfree, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_at_v, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_indep, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_none_of_rho_none}
At a variable $v$ that is free under $\rho_0$ and unset in both $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$,
the first component $\gamma$ of the encoder loop
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}'s \texttt{go} takes the same value at $v$ whether
started from $\sigma_1$ or $\sigma_2$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processEntries on processClauseLits data]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processEntries_of_processClauseLits}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
Characterizes the decoder's \texttt{processEntries} when run on the auxiliary entries of
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} followed by a termination marker $(w,
\mathrm{false})$ and a remainder: the result is given by folding the decoder's $\sigma$-
and $\rho_0$-updates over those auxiliary entries, leaving the remainder unconsumed,
provided $t.\mathrm{length} \le w$ and every input literal occurs in $t.\texttt{zipIdx}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[processClauseLits never sets $\sigma$ to the negating value]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_ne_neg}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits}
If no literal in the input list shares $l$'s variable except $l$ itself, and the initial
$\sigma$ at $l.\mathrm{var}$ is not $\mathrm{some}\ l.\mathrm{neg}$, then after
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} the $\sigma$ output at $l.\mathrm{var}$ is still
not $\mathrm{some}\ l.\mathrm{neg}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Path is exhausted when a member variable stays free]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_path_nil_of_rho_none_and_mem}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none}
Suppose $(l, idx)$ is in the input list, no other input literal shares $l$'s variable,
$\rho_0\,l.\mathrm{var} = \mathrm{none}$ initially, and
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} still leaves $\rho_0$ unset at
$l.\mathrm{var}$. Then the remaining path returned by the routine is empty, since $l$ was
never processed because the path ran out first.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[A member literal forces $\rho_0$ to be set]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none_of_mem}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none}
If some input pair $p$ has variable $v$ and there are at least as many path entries as
literals, then the $\rho_0$ output of \lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits} at $v$ is
not $\mathrm{none}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Round-trip base case]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.roundtrip_base}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
When the encoder returns $(\sigma, [])$ (an empty emitted list), the decoder loop
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovDecode}'s \texttt{go} recovers $\sigma$ exactly, given
that $\sigma$ and the decoder's starting restriction $\sigma_{\mathrm{dec}}$ agree with
$\sigma$ on both free and non-free variables and that $\sigma$ is unset wherever
$\rho_0$ is unset.
\end{lemma}
